benchmark_description: Tell a story or recount an experience with appropriate facts and relevant, descriptive details, speaking audibly in coherent sentences.

lesson.title: Speaking to Tell a Story: Facts, Details, and Clear Sentences
lesson.objective: Students will tell a story or recount an experience using relevant facts and descriptive details, speaking in clear, complete sentences that can be heard by the audience.
lesson.overview: When you tell a story or share an experience with an audience, you need to speak clearly, use facts and details, and organize your ideas in complete sentences. A good speaker helps the listener picture what happened and understand why it was important.
key_concepts: Audible: speaking loudly and clearly enough for everyone to hear
Coherent sentences: complete, organized sentences that make sense
Relevant details: details that fit the story and help the listener understand it
Descriptive details: words that help the listener picture what happened (colors, sounds, feelings)
Recounting: retelling something that happened in a clear, organized way

examples.label: Weak recounting
examples.text: We went somewhere. It was fun. Stuff happened.

examples.label: Strong recounting
examples.text: Last summer, my family hiked to the top of a mountain. The trail was steep and rocky, and I could hear the wind rushing through the pine trees. When we finally reached the top, I saw a stunning view of the whole valley below. I felt so proud that I had made it!

common_errors: Speaking too quietly for the audience to hear
Using vague words like 'stuff' and 'things' instead of specific descriptive details
Telling events out of order so the story is hard to follow
Speaking in incomplete sentences or fragments

practice_questions.prompt_md: When telling a story to your class, your voice should be ___.

choices.label: Very quiet so only one friend can hear
choices.explanation: If only one friend can hear, the audience is missing the story. Speak audibly.

choices.label: Loud enough for everyone in the room to hear
choices.explanation: Speaking audibly means loud and clear enough for the whole audience to hear you.

choices.label: Silent -- let someone else tell it
choices.explanation: The speaker must present the story. Staying silent is not presenting.

choices.label: As fast as possible so you finish quickly
choices.explanation: Speaking too fast makes it hard to understand. Speak at a clear, steady pace.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Audible** means speaking loudly and clearly enough for every listener to hear. Good speakers project their voice to the whole audience.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which sentence is a **complete, coherent sentence**?

choices.label: The dog.
choices.explanation: This is a fragment. It does not have a verb and does not express a complete thought.

choices.label: Ran so fast and then.
choices.explanation: This is a fragment. It has no subject and is not a complete thought.

choices.label: The dog ran so fast that he slid across the floor.
choices.explanation: This is a complete sentence with a subject (the dog), a verb (ran), and a complete thought.

choices.label: Fast dog.
choices.explanation: This is a fragment, not a complete sentence.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A **coherent sentence** has a subject and a verb and expresses a complete thought. Speak in complete sentences so your audience understands your story.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which detail is MOST relevant when recounting a story about your first day of school?

choices.label: My cousin has a cat.
choices.explanation: This detail has nothing to do with the first day of school.

choices.label: I felt nervous waiting in line to enter my new classroom.
choices.explanation: This detail about a feeling and a specific event is directly relevant to the first day of school story.

choices.label: My favorite food is macaroni and cheese.
choices.explanation: Food preferences are not relevant to the first day of school story.

choices.label: The weather last January was cold.
choices.explanation: This is not relevant to the first day of school story.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Relevant details** directly connect to the story. Choose details that help the listener understand what happened and why it matters.

practice_questions.prompt_md:
A student says: *'We went to the park. Things happened. It was nice.'*

What would make this recounting STRONGER?

choices.label: Speak even more quietly
choices.explanation: Speaking quietly makes the problem worse -- the listener cannot hear the details.

choices.label: Add descriptive details about what actually happened at the park
choices.explanation: Replacing vague words like 'Things happened' with specific, descriptive details would make the story clear and vivid.

choices.label: Leave out the part about the park
choices.explanation: The park is the topic of the story. Leaving it out makes the recounting incomplete.

choices.label: Use fewer sentences
choices.explanation: The recounting already has too little detail. Fewer sentences would make it even weaker.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Strong recount uses **descriptive details** -- specific words that help the listener picture the experience. Replace vague words like 'things' with what actually happened.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which recounting of a trip to the library is STRONGEST?

choices.label: We went to the library. It was good.
choices.explanation: No descriptive details and no relevant facts. Too vague.

choices.label: Library.
choices.explanation: A single-word fragment is not a recounting.

choices.label: Last Friday, our class walked to the city library. We each chose two books, and I picked a mystery about a girl who solves codes. The librarian read us a funny story and we laughed the whole time.
choices.explanation: This includes when (Last Friday), what happened (chose books, librarian read), specific details (mystery, codes, laughed), and complete sentences -- a strong recounting.

choices.label: We went somewhere and someone did something and it was fine.
choices.explanation: Extremely vague -- no relevant facts, no descriptive details, no specific events.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A strong recounting includes **when** it happened, **what** happened step by step, **specific details**, and **complete sentences** that the audience can clearly follow.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which detail is MOST descriptive when telling a story about a thunderstorm?

choices.label: It rained.
choices.explanation: This is a fact but has no descriptive detail. The listener cannot picture the storm.

choices.label: The thunder boomed like a drumroll and the lightning lit up my whole bedroom.
choices.explanation: This uses sound words (boomed, drumroll) and a visual detail (lit up the bedroom) -- vivid descriptive language.

choices.label: There was weather outside.
choices.explanation: Completely vague. 'Weather' gives no picture of the thunderstorm.

choices.label: I was inside.
choices.explanation: This is a relevant fact but has no descriptive detail.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Descriptive details** use specific sensory words (sounds, sights, feelings) that help the listener picture the experience as if they were there.

practice_questions.prompt_md: When presenting a story to the class, you should look ___.

choices.label: Down at the floor the whole time
choices.explanation: Looking at the floor makes it hard to connect with the audience and your voice may not project well.

choices.label: At your audience
choices.explanation: Eye contact with the audience shows confidence and helps listeners feel engaged with your story.

choices.label: Out the window
choices.explanation: Looking out the window shows you are distracted and not focused on your audience.

choices.label: At only one person the entire time
choices.explanation: Good speakers share eye contact with the whole audience, not just one person.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Eye contact** with the audience is an important speaking skill. It shows confidence, keeps listeners engaged, and helps your voice project clearly.

practice_questions.prompt_md: A student is telling a story about winning a race. Which sentence belongs in the MIDDLE of the story?

choices.label: Last week, my school held a field day.
choices.explanation: This introduces the event. It belongs at the beginning.

choices.label: As I rounded the last curve, my legs burned and I pushed harder than ever.
choices.explanation: This is a middle-of-the-story detail describing the action and feelings during the race.

choices.label: Winning the race was the proudest moment of my year.
choices.explanation: This reflects back on the event. It belongs at the end as closure.

choices.label: My name is Tayla and I like to run.
choices.explanation: This is an introduction about the speaker. It belongs at the beginning, not the middle.

practice_questions.explanation_md: When recounting, organize events logically: **beginning** (introduce), **middle** (tell the main action with descriptive details), **end** (closure/reflection).

practice_questions.prompt_md:
Read this recounting:

*'On Monday, our class planted seeds in paper cups. First, I poured dark brown soil into my cup. Next, I pressed one tiny seed into the center with my finger. Then our teacher watered each cup gently. I hope the seeds grow into tall sunflowers!'*

What makes this recounting strong?

choices.label: It uses opinions instead of facts.
choices.explanation: The recounting uses facts (soil, seed, watered) -- not pure opinions.

choices.label: It has temporal words, descriptive details, and coherent sentences.
choices.explanation: Temporal words (First, Next, Then), descriptive details (dark brown soil, tiny seed, pressed with my finger), and complete sentences make this a strong recounting.

choices.label: It has no organization and jumps around.
choices.explanation: The recounting is clearly organized with temporal words. It does not jump around.

choices.label: It is too short to be a good recounting.
choices.explanation: Quality matters more than length. This short recounting is detailed and well-organized.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A strong recounting uses **temporal words** (first, next, then), **descriptive details** (specific sensory words), and **coherent sentences** that the audience can follow.

practice_questions.prompt_md: A student wants to recount making pancakes with her grandmother. Which plan would produce the STRONGEST recounting?

choices.label: Say 'We made pancakes. They were good.' and sit down.
choices.explanation: Two vague sentences with no details, temporal words, or complete story -- the weakest approach.

choices.label: List every ingredient they used without saying what happened.
choices.explanation: A list of ingredients is not a recounting. It has no events, feelings, or story arc.

choices.label: Organize events in order with temporal words, add specific details about smells and feelings, and speak in complete sentences.
choices.explanation: Organizing events, using temporal words, adding sensory details (smells, feelings), and speaking in complete sentences produces the strongest recounting.

choices.label: Speak as fast as possible so she finishes before the timer goes off.
choices.explanation: Speed makes it hard to understand. A strong recounting is paced clearly, not rushed.

practice_questions.explanation_md: The strongest recounting plan: organize events with **temporal words**, include **sensory and emotional details**, and speak in **clear, complete sentences** at a pace the audience can follow.

mastery_check.prompt_md: Which detail is MOST relevant to a recounting about your first time swimming?

choices.label: Swimming pools were invented a long time ago.
choices.explanation: Historical facts about pools are not relevant to your personal first-time swimming experience.

choices.label: I took a deep breath, jumped in, and felt the cool water surround me.
choices.explanation: This is directly relevant. It describes the physical and sensory experience of jumping in for the first time.

choices.label: My favorite movie is about a mermaid.
choices.explanation: Your favorite movie is not relevant to your first swimming experience.

choices.label: Water is made of hydrogen and oxygen.
choices.explanation: A chemistry fact is not a relevant detail for a personal narrative recounting.

mastery_check.explanation_md: **Relevant details** connect directly to the story being told. Sensory and emotional details about the experience itself are the most relevant.

mastery_check.prompt_md: Which sentence uses the MOST descriptive language to tell about seeing a firework show?

choices.label: There were fireworks.
choices.explanation: No descriptive detail. The listener gets no picture of the fireworks.

choices.label: Fireworks went off.
choices.explanation: Still vague -- no sensory details.

choices.label: Fireworks exploded in bursts of red, gold, and purple that lit up the whole night sky.
choices.explanation: Colors (red, gold, purple), action (exploded), and scope (lit up the whole night sky) -- rich descriptive language.

choices.label: We saw some things in the sky.
choices.explanation: 'Some things' is the most vague description possible -- completely lacking detail.

mastery_check.explanation_md: **Descriptive language** uses specific sensory words -- colors, sounds, sizes -- that help the audience picture exactly what happened.

mastery_check.prompt_md:
A student's recounting of a class science experiment says: *'We put baking soda in the bottle. Then we added vinegar. It fizzed up like crazy and smelled like sour lemons.'*

What makes the last sentence the STRONGEST part?

choices.label: It uses the word 'crazy' which is funny.
choices.explanation: While vivid, the real strength is the sensory details, not just one word.

choices.label: It adds sensory details (sight and smell) that help the audience picture the reaction.
choices.explanation: 'Fizzed up' gives a visual, and 'smelled like sour lemons' gives a smell -- two sensory details that make the experience vivid.

choices.label: It tells the audience the experiment failed.
choices.explanation: Fizzing up is what was supposed to happen -- the experiment succeeded.

choices.label: It uses a temporal word.
choices.explanation: The last sentence has no temporal word. The temporal word 'Then' is in the previous sentence.

mastery_check.explanation_md: The strongest descriptive details appeal to the **senses** -- what you see, hear, smell, taste, or feel. Multi-sensory details create the most vivid picture for the listener.

remediation.prompt_md: When you speak to your class, you should speak ___.

choices.label: So softly nobody can hear
choices.explanation: If nobody can hear you, your story is not being shared. Speak louder.

choices.label: Loudly and clearly so everyone can hear
choices.explanation: Speaking loudly and clearly (audibly) makes sure the whole audience can enjoy your story.

choices.label: As fast as possible
choices.explanation: Speed makes it hard to understand. Pace yourself so the audience can follow.

choices.label: In a whisper
choices.explanation: Whispering is too quiet for a class presentation.

remediation.explanation_md: **Audible** speaking means everyone can hear you clearly. Project your voice to the back of the room.

choices.label: The big red.
choices.explanation: This is a fragment. It has no verb and no complete idea.

choices.label: My sister won the spelling bee.
choices.explanation: This is a complete sentence. It has a subject (My sister), a verb (won), and a complete thought.

choices.label: Won the
choices.explanation: This is a fragment -- incomplete.

choices.label: Because she studied.
choices.explanation: This is a dependent clause, not a complete sentence on its own.

remediation.explanation_md: A **complete sentence** has a subject and a verb and expresses a full thought. Speak in complete sentences when recounting a story.

remediation.prompt_md: You are telling the class about your birthday party. Which detail should you include?

choices.label: My neighbor's phone number
choices.explanation: This is not relevant to the birthday party story.

choices.label: We played freeze tag and I won three rounds in a row!
choices.explanation: This detail is directly relevant -- it describes a specific event at the party.

choices.label: My teacher's favorite book
choices.explanation: This has nothing to do with your birthday party.

choices.label: The history of birthday cakes
choices.explanation: Historical background is not relevant to recounting your personal birthday party.

remediation.explanation_md: Include **relevant details** -- things that actually happened at your party and help the audience picture the experience.

remediation.prompt_md: Which recounting sentence has the MOST descriptive detail?

choices.label: The food was good.
choices.explanation: No specific details. 'Good' does not help the listener picture the food.

choices.label: We ate food.
choices.explanation: Completely vague -- no descriptive detail at all.

choices.label: We bit into crispy tacos loaded with melted cheese and tangy salsa.
choices.explanation: Crispy, melted, tangy -- these descriptive words let the listener taste and picture the food.

choices.label: There was something to eat.
choices.explanation: Extremely vague. 'Something' gives no detail.

remediation.explanation_md: **Descriptive details** use specific words for colors, textures, tastes, sounds, and feelings. Replace vague words with specific ones.

remediation.prompt_md: What should you do FIRST when getting ready to share a story with the class?

choices.label: Think about what happened in order and what details you want to share
choices.explanation: Organizing your thoughts -- what happened, in what order, and what details matter -- is the first step before speaking.

choices.label: Start talking immediately without thinking
choices.explanation: Speaking without organizing your thoughts often produces a confusing, out-of-order story.

choices.label: Read from a book
choices.explanation: Recounting is about telling your own story, not reading from a book.

choices.label: Ask someone else to tell your story
choices.explanation: You are the speaker. Organize your thoughts and share your own story.

remediation.explanation_md: Before presenting, **organize your story**: what happened first? Next? What details will help the audience picture it? Planning makes your recounting clear.
